IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—accurate morpheme segmentation—by introducing a novel combination of orthographic rules with vocabulary-wide semantic signals (difference-vector consistency), which is the first framework to validate affix rules using such syntactico-semantic information. Introducing the SD17 dataset to correct evaluation shortcomings in Morpho Challenge and demonstrating state-of-the-art gains on English and Turkish make it likely to influence future work; however, practical limits (concatenative-only morphology, greedy inference error propagation, and weaker Finnish results) constrain its immediacy as a transformative change, placing it as a strong-impact contribution that should spur new research directions. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem—reducing over-segmentation in morpheme segmentation—by introducing a novel combination of orthographic rules and vocabulary-wide semantic signals (difference-vector consistency), which demonstrably improves results (state-of-the-art on English and Turkish) and offers a new methodological perspective. The release of a semantically-driven dataset (SD17) that addresses compositionality shortcomings in Morpho Challenge is likely to change evaluation practices and motivate follow-up work. Limitations (worse performance on Finnish, restriction to concatenative morphology, and greedy inference) temper its reach but do not negate its clear influence on how researchers incorporate semantic information into morphological analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—accurate morpheme segmentation—by introducing a novel combination of orthographic rules with vocabulary-wide semantic signals (difference-vector consistency), which is the first framework to validate affix rules using such syntactico-semantic information. Introducing the SD17 dataset to correct evaluation shortcomings in Morpho Challenge and demonstrating state-of-the-art gains on English and Turkish make it likely to influence future work; however, practical limits (concatenative-only morphology, greedy inference error propagation, and weaker Finnish results) constrain its immediacy as a transformative change, placing it as a strong-impact contribution that should spur new research directions.

The release of a semantically-driven dataset (SD17) that addresses compositionality shortcomings in Morpho Challenge is likely to change evaluation practices and motivate follow-up work. Limitations (worse performance on Finnish, restriction to concatenative morphology, and greedy inference) temper its reach but do not negate its clear influence on how researchers incorporate semantic information into morphological analysis.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs multilingual experiments (English, Turkish, Finnish) and evaluates on both Morpho Challenge and a newly introduced SD17 dataset, which supports the main claims about semantic validation and exposes shortcomings of prior benchmarks. It reports hyperparameters and a sensitivity analysis for gold-tuning set size (performance stabilizes at ~300), and discusses limitations (greedy inference, concatenative-only scope, worse Finnish results). Related work coverage is described as thorough. However, it is unclear from the summary whether explicit ablation studies isolating semantic vs. orthographic contributions or released code/datasets for full reproducibility are provided, so some experimental details remain missing. | B(4): The paper runs comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets (Morpho Challenge and the new SD17, including canonical/non-canonical splits), compares to several strong baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, Lignos), and reports language-specific results (English, Turkish, Finnish). It reports hyperparameters and a sensitivity check for gold-tuning size (performance stabilizes at ~300 examples), and discusses important limitations (greedy inference, restriction to concatenative morphology, need for a small tuning set). Related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor gap is that code availability is not mentioned, so reproducibility is only partially supported despite reported hyperparameters.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs multilingual experiments (English, Turkish, Finnish) and evaluates on both Morpho Challenge and a newly introduced SD17 dataset, which supports the main claims about semantic validation and exposes shortcomings of prior benchmarks. It reports hyperparameters and a sensitivity analysis for gold-tuning set size (performance stabilizes at ~300), and discusses limitations (greedy inference, concatenative-only scope, worse Finnish results). Related work coverage is described as thorough. However, it is unclear from the summary whether explicit ablation studies isolating semantic vs. orthographic contributions or released code/datasets for full reproducibility are provided, so some experimental details remain missing.

It reports hyperparameters and a sensitivity check for gold-tuning size (performance stabilizes at ~300 examples), and discusses important limitations (greedy inference, restriction to concatenative morphology, need for a small tuning set). Related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor gap is that code availability is not mentioned, so reproducibility is only partially supported despite reported hyperparameters.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodology section that follows naturally from the introduction (orthographic rule discovery -> semantic validation -> greedy inference) and comprehensive experiments on multiple datasets (MC, SD17, canonical/non-canonical). The main contributions are stated early and supported by comparisons to several baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, Base Inference) and evaluation metrics (precision/recall/F1, boundary- and morpheme-level). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are described (hyperparameters reported; related work coverage thorough), and the writing quality is noted as good, though the summary does not provide explicit evidence about the effectiveness of individual figures/tables or whether any minor presentation polish is needed, so I award a 4 rather than a 5. | B(4): The paper is well-organized (clear progression from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion) and states its main contributions early (MORSE framework and the SD17 dataset). Notation appears consistent and the methodology, baselines, datasets, and hyperparameters are clearly reported; figures/tables and examples are informative. Writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough, with only minor presentation issues (e.g., limitations like greedy inference and the small gold tuning set are noted but could use slightly more exposition).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodology section that follows naturally from the introduction (orthographic rule discovery -> semantic validation -> greedy inference) and comprehensive experiments on multiple datasets (MC, SD17, canonical/non-canonical). The main contributions are stated early and supported by comparisons to several baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, Base Inference) and evaluation metrics (precision/recall/F1, boundary- and morpheme-level). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are described (hyperparameters reported; related work coverage thorough), and the writing quality is noted as good, though the summary does not provide explicit evidence about the effectiveness of individual figures/tables or whether any minor presentation polish is needed, so I award a 4 rather than a 5.

Writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough, with only minor presentation issues (e.g., limitations like greedy inference and the small gold tuning set are noted but could use slightly more exposition).

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: morpheme segmentation using learned semantic signals and embeddings is a core NLP task leveraging ML methods. The paper uses methods and evaluation standards expected at the venue (difference-vector consistency with word embeddings, orthographic rule induction), compares to strong baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, Base Inference), and evaluates on standard (Morpho Challenge) and newly introduced SD17 datasets with precision/recall/F1 and boundary-level metrics. Although the authors transparently note limitations (greedy inference, restriction to concatenative morphology and weaker results on Finnish), the problem formulation, audience, and experimental positioning are appropriate for a top-tier conference. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP task (morpheme segmentation) and introduces a novel, semantically-driven approach combining orthographic rules with embedding-based semantic validation, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It proposes a new dataset (SD17) to fix benchmark shortcomings, compares against standard baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM), and uses accepted evaluation metrics (precision/recall/F1, boundary- and morpheme-level), demonstrating state-of-the-art results on multiple languages—indicating the right audience and evaluation standards. While it notes sensible limitations (concatenative morphology, greedy inference, small tuning set), the work is well positioned and relevant to the venue’s research community.

While it notes sensible limitations (concatenative morphology, greedy inference, small tuning set), the work is well positioned and relevant to the venue’s research community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM (Narasimhan et al., 2015), Base Inference (Lignos, 2010)) and evaluates on shared benchmarks (Morpho Challenge) while also introducing SD17 to address MC shortcomings. Evaluation uses standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, boundary- and morpheme-level) and reports hyperparameters and tuning data, indicating fair conditions. The authors acknowledge when prior methods outperform theirs (worse on Finnish) and analyze how their semantic signals reduce over-segmentation compared to baselines. Related work coverage is described as thorough, supporting that relevant competing approaches were cited and discussed. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Morfessor and its variants, LLSM, Base Inference) and evaluates on common benchmarks (Morpho Challenge) as well as a new SD17 dataset using standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, boundary- and morpheme-level). It reports hyperparameters and discusses limitations, and explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods are better (worse performance on Finnish). Related work coverage is described as thorough, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant approaches.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper brings a notable novel element by combining orthographic rule mining with vocabulary-wide semantic validation using difference-vector consistency—claimed as the first framework to use syntactico-semantic signals to validate affix rules—and by introducing the SD17 dataset to better capture compositionality issues in evaluation. These contributions clearly differentiate it from prior work like Morfessor and LLSM and represent a non-trivial extension (semantic difference-vector consistency + embedding-based validation). Because morpheme segmentation is a well-studied problem and the work largely augments existing pipeline approaches rather than proposing a wholly new paradigm, the contribution is notably novel but not revolutionary. | B(4): The paper presents a notable novel approach by combining orthographic affix-rule learning with vocabulary-wide semantic validation using difference-vector consistency, which (per the authors) is the first framework to use syntactico-semantic information to validate morphological rules. It also contributes a new semantically-driven dataset (SD17) to address compositionality shortcomings in existing benchmarks. These elements clearly differentiate it from prior work such as Morfessor and LLSM and constitute a non-trivial extension rather than a simple recombination of existing techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper brings a notable novel element by combining orthographic rule mining with vocabulary-wide semantic validation using difference-vector consistency—claimed as the first framework to use syntactico-semantic signals to validate affix rules—and by introducing the SD17 dataset to better capture compositionality issues in evaluation. These contributions clearly differentiate it from prior work like Morfessor and LLSM and represent a non-trivial extension (semantic difference-vector consistency + embedding-based validation). Because morpheme segmentation is a well-studied problem and the work largely augments existing pipeline approaches rather than proposing a wholly new paradigm, the contribution is notably novel but not revolutionary.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable novel approach by combining orthographic affix-rule learning with vocabulary-wide semantic validation using difference-vector consistency, which (per the authors) is the first framework to use syntactico-semantic information to validate morphological rules. It also contributes a new semantically-driven dataset (SD17) to address compositionality shortcomings in existing benchmarks. These elements clearly differentiate it from prior work such as Morfessor and LLSM and constitute a non-trivial extension rather than a simple recombination of existing techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s central claims—that combining orthographic rules with vocabulary-wide semantic validation (difference-vector consistency) improves morpheme segmentation and that a new SD17 dataset better captures compositionality—are supported by experimental comparisons to reasonable baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM) and reported gains on English and Turkish. Mathematical aspects appear lightweight and appropriate for the approach, and the methodology includes sensible controls (multiple baselines and a new dataset). However, there are minor technical concerns: no mention of statistical significance testing, reliance on pre-trained embeddings (which may vary across languages), and a greedy inference scheme whose error propagation the authors acknowledge; the poorer Finnish result also indicates limited generality. Overall the evidence supports the main conclusions, but some robustness and generality issues remain. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are backed by experimental evidence (performance gains on English and Turkish, new SD17 dataset addressing Morpho Challenge shortcomings) and a clear methodology combining orthographic rules with semantic validation. There are no mathematical derivations to verify, and the methodological setup uses appropriate baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, etc.), but the summary does not report statistical significance tests or publicly available code, and the reliance on a small gold tuning set and greedy inference are acknowledged limitations that slightly weaken conclusiveness. Overall the technical approach and reported results are sound with only minor issues that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s central claims—that combining orthographic rules with vocabulary-wide semantic validation (difference-vector consistency) improves morpheme segmentation and that a new SD17 dataset better captures compositionality—are supported by experimental comparisons to reasonable baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM) and reported gains on English and Turkish. Mathematical aspects appear lightweight and appropriate for the approach, and the methodology includes sensible controls (multiple baselines and a new dataset). However, there are minor technical concerns: no mention of statistical significance testing, reliance on pre-trained embeddings (which may vary across languages), and a greedy inference scheme whose error propagation the authors acknowledge; the poorer Finnish result also indicates limited generality. Overall the evidence supports the main conclusions, but some robustness and generality issues remain.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are backed by experimental evidence (performance gains on English and Turkish, new SD17 dataset addressing Morpho Challenge shortcomings) and a clear methodology combining orthographic rules with semantic validation. There are no mathematical derivations to verify, and the methodological setup uses appropriate baselines (Morfessor variants, LLSM, etc.), but the summary does not report statistical significance tests or publicly available code, and the reliance on a small gold tuning set and greedy inference are acknowledged limitations that slightly weaken conclusiveness. Overall the technical approach and reported results are sound with only minor issues that do not overturn the main conclusions.

main_claims: MORSE combines orthographic and semantic information (using word representations and difference vectors) to reduce over-segmentation in morpheme segmentation.
MORSE is the first framework to consider vocabulary-wide syntactico-semantic information (difference-vector consistency) to validate morphological affix rules.

The paper introduces a semantically-driven dataset (SD17) to address shortcomings of existing benchmarks (Morpho Challenge) with respect to compositionality, and demonstrates that MC underestimates the performance gap.

MORSE achieves state-of-the-art results, outperforming Morfessor on English and Turkish (but performing worse on Finnish) and surpassing several published baselines including LLSM and Morfessor variants.
_summary.methodology: Learn candidate orthographic affix transformation rules from word pairs, validate/invalidate rules and word pairs using semantic signals from word embeddings (difference-vector consistency and semantic relatedness) plus orthographic scores, then greedily segment words by searching rules that maximize combined semantic/orthographic scores under threshold constraints.
datasets: canonical and non-canonical versions of SD17
a publicly released 100-word English non-segmentable (compositionality) collection
limitations: Greedy inference: early segmentation errors propagate and hurt subsequent segmentations.
Limited to concatenative morphology, reducing efficacy on languages with non-concatenative morphology.
Requires a small gold tuning set for hyperparameter tuning (though authors report minimal dependency, reaching steady performance at ~300 gold segmentations).
Performs worse than Morfessor on Finnish in the reported experiments.
